paragraphs.body: An Old Cola bottle My dad called me downstairs for something. I figured he’d be sitting in his office, so I quickly headed down the old, carpeted stairs. A loud creaking sound with every step I took. I Walked down the dark hall to his office, feeling the cold, uneven white tiles under my bare feet. I grabbed the door handle and slowly cracked the door open, expecting to be greeted by my dad’s warm smile. To my surprise, he wasn’t in the room. I was about to walk out when something caught my eye. It came from the shelf across the room. I have always noticed this shelve in my dad’s office because it was filled with little nick-nacks that my dad has collected throughout his whole life. Each object seemed like a miniature artifact, telling the story of his past. I made my way across the room, I was hit with the nostalgic smell of leather-backed books. As I filtered through the array of my dad’s safekeeping, I noticed a few things that included a green hat my great grampa wore when in the military, I noticed a little toy airplane, and multiple law books that my dad would read to me as a baby when he was still in school. I finally noticed what had previously caught my eye. A little 1990s Coca-Cola bottle covered with a gray blanket of dust. I picked up the bottle and used my shirt to wipe it clean. I carefully looked at the molasses-colored liquid inside that shimmered faintly in the dim light. I couldn’t help but wonder what it tasted like. The sweet, tangy carbonated beverage that fizzes out of a can when you open it on a sunny day couldn’t be the same as this dusty bottle that’s sat on my dad’s shelf for more than 20 years. But yet, it looked as though it had been untouched by time. Besides the bottle being covered with dust, it didn’t look like it’s seen more than a week. I began to think about how we all hold onto Josephine 2 pieces of our past, small fragments of memory that we keep tucked away in the corners of our lives. Just like that bottle, we all have parts of ourselves that are unaltered by time.

annotations.text: Each object seemed like a miniature artifact, telling the story of his past.

The sweet, tangy carbonated beverage that fizzes out of a can when you open it on a sunny day couldn’t be the same as this dusty bottle that’s sat on my dad’s shelf for more than 20 years. But yet, it looked as though it had been untouched by time.

annotations.text: Just like that bottle, we all have parts of ourselves that are unaltered by time.
annotations.comment: I don't think you need this line. The sentence before it is a beauty and does all the work you need it to.

paragraphs.body: I looked closely at the bottle and its unchanged contents. The bottle was frozen in time, untouched by the hand of inevitable aging. It sits quietly on the shelf, watching days, months, and years pass it by. We always keep a little bit of our past, a word, phrase, object, or smell can trigger memories and parts of ourselves that we can’t quite shake away. Words that never age in our minds hold the same unaged memories. Just like that bottle, I was caught in a moment in time, unable to break free. Suddenly, I was sent back in time where I had that same feeling of being trapped. A part of my past that has been untouched, unshaken, from the aging of time. A part of my past unable to break free from the stillness that clung to me.

paragraphs.body: On a bland Tuesday in February, I walked into my high school cafeteria and was immediately hit with the unsettling aroma of warmed-up frozen pizza and old fruit. I hurriedly looked for somewhere to sit, I found an empty seat and promptly sat myself down at the end of the table. A few minutes later, I found myself dozing off, twisting my napkin into little knots on my lap. Something I often found myself doing when I was anxious. The buzz of the cafeteria, all the laughter, the moving of chairs, and the scraping of trays were all quiet compared to the tightness I felt in my chest. I longingly looked over at the girls I sit next to every day, wanting to talk and laugh with them. I noticed their eyes darting over to my direction and the sneaky whispers they exchanged between themselves. I quickly focused my gaze on my lunch box, pretending to ravage through it while trying to listen to them. I overheard them saying, “She's always so quiet why doesn’t she say anything? She’s like a robot.” I immediately knew they were talking about me. The words stung more than they should have. My face started to burn. I turned my head, hoping they wouldn’t notice my cheeks turn red and my eyes welling up. I was Josephine 3 embarrassed at the amount of emotion it brought out of me. I turned back and faced them, forcing a smile in hopes of deterring them from any ongoing conversation about me. It’s not that I didn’t have anything to say. I sank shyly into my chair and folded my hands on my lap. I hated having the reputation of being quiet or standoffish, but I was content with being quiet it was just who I was. Maybe I think it’s peaceful to sit in my silence. Maybe I liked the calming aspect of being quiet without having to be judged for it. Why is it expected to always have something to say? why am I being labeled as weird or emotionless just for minding my own business?

paragraphs.body: Being quiet is not ignoring the person who sits next to you in class. It’s not saying the least number of things possible to make you think I don’t like you. Being quiet isn’t because I'm sad. It doesn’t mean I'm in a bad mood. Quiet is not being weak or that I'm insecure. It's not about having nothing to say but rather choosing when and how to speak. Sometimes, the words are tangled up, unable to break free, and in these moments, quiet can be a shield. Being quiet is a refuge from the chaos of the world. Being quiet can give you a chance to reflect. It allows you to observe, listen, and understand what goes on around you. Quiet is seeing the small details and recognizing when a friend is having a bad day. As someone who’s been voted the quietest in my grade from my class, I feel as though I'm qualified to speak on this subject. Through my experiences, I’ve learned that silence can be both a gift and a challenge. People will judge and always have something to say about you. but in the end, silence, to me, is peace and contentment. it’s about finding calm in the stillness and choosing my words when I feel they matter most.

annotations.text: People will judge and always have something to say about you. but in the end, silence, to me, is peace and contentment. it’s about finding calm in the stillness and choosing my words when I feel they matter most.
annotations.comment: Related to your final question, which is something about not staying trapped in others' perceptions of you, but learning how to redefine those perceptions for yourself as strengths.e

paragraphs.body: I can still feel everything I did that day in the cafeteria like it was preserved in a time capsule. Silently sitting there trapped in others' perceptions of me. “The quiet girl” label stuck to me like a leach. The sting of the girl’s words was more than just embarrassment it was about being misunderstood and enclosed by a world full of expectations. I felt like that bottle, stuck in Josephine 4 time, collecting dust, watching the days go by. As I sat there looking at the bottle, I felt a deeper connection to it than I would have expected. It’s a reminder that parts of me and my identity are frozen in time. I thought of my dad as a young, hopeful boy collecting this bottle, unaware of the vast future ahead of him. Yet despite all of that, his Coca-Cola bottle still finds a place on his shelf, unchanging and untouched, much like my memories and parts of myself I hold onto that no longer serve me any good. I wonder if there is a way to let go of the past without letting go of the lesson. Leaving behind what I used to describe myself for so long. Perhaps the bottle symbolizes not the passage of time but the opportunity of today and the fact that you can take control and move forward. What’s holding you frozen in time?

annotations.text: Silently sitting there trapped in others' perceptions of me.
annotations.comment: This idea seems important--lots of heat and energy here. That even though things happened a long time ago they still effect the ways we see ourselves now?
